evidence: Package layout follows a clean layered hierarchy: api -> exploration -> query -> model/utils, with config consumed at multiple layers but never in a bottom-up direction
QueryExecutor interface in query/ properly abstracts the BlackLab implementation; ExplorationHandlers depends only on the interface, not BlackLabQueryExecutor directly
CollocateQueryHelper is package-private within query/, correctly constraining its blast radius

api/ package uses com.sun.net.httpserver (a non-public JDK internal API) in all 5 files: WordSketchApiServer, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, HttpApiUtils — cross-cutting dependency on a private API

BlackLabQueryExecutor imports ~15 distinct BlackLab types directly (BlackLab, BlackLabIndex, BLSpanQuery, ContextualQueryLanguageParser, CorpusQueryLanguageParser, HitGroups, etc), making the query/ package a hub tightly coupled to the library's internal type surface
No dependency cycles detected across packages — dependency graph is acyclic
ExplorationHandlers calls result.getEdges() which is @Deprecated in ExplorationResult in favor of buildEdges() — caller is not respecting the published boundary change
LongIntHashMap in utils/ is not referenced in any production package beyond tests — may be an unused utility that inflates the utils boundary without serving a current need

strengths: Clean layered package hierarchy with no detected cross-package cycles
QueryExecutor interface properly decouples api/exploration layers from the BlackLab implementation
CollocateQueryHelper scoped package-private, limiting its blast radius to query/ only
model/ package is a pure data layer with no upward dependencies
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at the api/infrastructure boundary — the private JDK HTTP API bleeds across all api/ files, and the query/ package holds tight library coupling rather than wrapping it behind a thinner facade.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has a sensible and consistent layered structure that would be immediately navigable by a new engineer. Dependency directions are clean. The two structural concerns — the private JDK HTTP server API pervading api/ and the heavy BlackLab import surface in query/ — reduce resilience against library and JDK changes, but they do not cause cross-layer pollution or cycles. This places the codebase above a 'significant drag' baseline but below 'solid and consistent' because one infrastructure choice (sun.net.httpserver) is load-bearing across the entire HTTP layer with no abstraction.

issues.summary: api/ package uses com.sun.net.httpserver (private JDK API) in all 5 files with no wrapper
evidence: WordSketchApiServer.java line 41: private final com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer server;
ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, and HttpApiUtils all import com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange directly
There is no internal HttpHandler or HttpExchange adapter/wrapper — handler signatures expose the private type directly
issues.suggestion: Introduce a thin internal request/response abstraction (e.g. a RequestContext record) so that handler logic can be tested without the JDK private type. This also isolates the blast radius if the HTTP server is ever swapped. As a minimum, consolidate the HttpServer creation in WordSketchApiServer and avoid re-importing the JDK private type in every handler file.

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers calls the @Deprecated result.getEdges() instead of buildEdges()
evidence: ExplorationResult.java line 83-87: @Deprecated public List<Edge> getEdges() { return buildEdges(); } — with explicit deprecation notice naming buildEdges() as the replacement
issues.suggestion: Replace result.getEdges() with result.buildEdges() in ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison; the null check can also be removed since buildEdges() never returns null.

root_causes: The project bootstrapped with the JDK's built-in HTTP server for simplicity; because no abstraction was placed around it early, the private API type propagated into all handler classes
BlackLab's rich type surface is consumed directly in the executor rather than being wrapped in a narrower adapter class
